title: Choosing K and the denoising parameters
text: Choosing K single consequential decision MetaHunt fit. Picking K small underfits: real cross-study heterogeneity gets squashed low-rank approximation represent data, downstream predictions biased. Picking K large inflates variance risks recovering spurious “bases” fit noise. denoising step d-fSPA controls finite-sample variance complementary way: averaging study near neighbours basis hunting smooths per-study estimation error, cost small smoothing bias.

title: Choosing K and the denoising parameters
code: m <- 30; G <- 20; K_true <- 3 x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = G) basis <- rbind(sin(pi * x), cos(pi * x), x) W <- data.frame(w1 = rnorm(m), w2 = rnorm(m)) beta <- cbind(c(1, -0.8), c(-0.5, 1.2), c(0, 0)) pi_true <- exp(as.matrix(W) %*% beta); pi_true <- pi_true / rowSums(pi_true) F_hat <- pi_true %*% basis + matrix(rnorm(m * G, sd = 0.05), m, G)

title: Conformal prediction with different choices
code: # m = 80 is large enough that with cal_frac = 0.5 and alpha = 0.05 the conformal quantile is finite. m <- 80; G <- 20; K_true <- 3 x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = G) basis <- rbind(sin(pi * x), cos(pi * x), x) W <- data.frame(w1 = rnorm(m), w2 = rnorm(m)) beta <- cbind(c(1, -0.8), c(-0.5, 1.2), c(0, 0)) pi_true <- exp(as.matrix(W) %*% beta); pi_true <- pi_true / rowSums(pi_true) F_hat <- pi_true %*% basis + matrix(rnorm(m * G, sd = 0.05), m, G) W_new <- data.frame(w1 = c(0, 1, -1), w2 = c(0, -0.5, 1))

title: Preparing your data: from fitted models to F_hat
text: keep tutorial fully reproducible without external dependencies, complete onramp using lm() per-centre model. (lm-onramp flow previously appeared introductory vignette, broken annotated.) now F_hat_centres (m × G_grid) centre_meta (m × p), everything metahunt() needs.
code: m <- 8 centre_meta <- data.frame(   region     = factor(sample(c("N", "S", "E", "W"), m, replace = TRUE)),   mean_age   = round(runif(m, 50, 70)),   pct_female = round(runif(m, 0.4, 0.6), 2) )  # Each centre fits a quadratic on a single covariate `x`. make_centre_data <- function(i) {   x <- runif(80, -1, 1)   beta <- centre_meta$mean_age[i] / 60      # toy effect of metadata   data.frame(x = x, y = beta * x + 0.3 * x^2 + rnorm(80, sd = 0.2)) } centre_models <- lapply(seq_len(m), function(i)   stats::lm(y ~ poly(x, 2), data = make_centre_data(i)))  # A 1-D grid in the centres' covariate space. grid_centres  <- data.frame(x = seq(-1, 1, length.out = 30)) F_hat_centres <- f_hat_from_models(centre_models, grid_centres)  dim(F_hat_centres)        # 8 x 30 #> [1]  8 30 F_hat_centres[1:3, 1:5] #>            [,1]       [,2]       [,3]       [,4]       [,5] #> [1,] -0.6884554 -0.6575489 -0.6241455 -0.5882450 -0.5498475 #> [2,] -0.6822055 -0.6615548 -0.6371818 -0.6090865 -0.5772689 #> [3,] -0.6552212 -0.6306971 -0.6031561 -0.5725983 -0.5390236

what: predict_fn for custom S4 / bespoke classes
title: Preparing your data: from fitted models to F_hat
text: fitted models S4 objects, ensembles, otherwise need custom handling, override dispatcher predict_fn. function must accept (model, grid) return length-nrow(grid) numeric vector: pattern extends S4 model objects: write one-line adapter pulls predictions coerces numeric vector.
code: # Toy "model" that is just a list with a slope. predict_fn evaluates it. fake_models <- lapply(seq_len(4), function(i)   list(slope = i / 4, intercept = 0))  custom_predict <- function(model, grid) {   model$intercept + model$slope * grid$x }  F_hat_custom <- f_hat_from_models(fake_models, grid_centres,                                   predict_fn = custom_predict) dim(F_hat_custom)         # 4 x 30 #> [1]  4 30

title: Preparing your data: from fitted models to F_hat
text: grid need one-dimensional. one patient-level covariate, sample grid multivariate reference dataset let predict() evaluate row. number grid points G_grid choose. Larger grids give finer-resolution function estimates; smaller grids run faster. dozen hundred typical.
code: # A 3-covariate reference dataset and a sub-sampled grid. ref3   <- data.frame(age = rnorm(400, 60, 10),                      bp  = rnorm(400, 130, 15),                      bmi = rnorm(400, 28,  4)) grid3  <- build_grid(ref3, n_grid = 25, seed = 1) dim(grid3) #> [1] 25  3  # Each centre fits an lm on (age, bp, bmi); slopes vary across centres # so there is genuine cross-centre heterogeneity to recover. set.seed(2) m3 <- 8 centre_data3 <- lapply(seq_len(m3), function(i) {   n_i <- 60   age <- rnorm(n_i, 60, 10)   bp  <- rnorm(n_i, 130, 15)   bmi <- rnorm(n_i, 28, 4)   # slopes vary across centres   beta_age <- 0.02 + 0.03 * (i / m3)   beta_bp  <- -0.01 + 0.02 * cos(pi * i / m3)   beta_bmi <- 0.05 - 0.04 * (i / m3)   y <- beta_age * age + beta_bp * bp + beta_bmi * bmi + rnorm(n_i, sd = 0.3)   data.frame(age = age, bp = bp, bmi = bmi, y = y) }) centre_models3 <- lapply(centre_data3, function(d) stats::lm(y ~ age + bp + bmi, data = d))  F_hat3 <- f_hat_from_models(centre_models3, grid3) dim(F_hat3)               # 8 x 25 #> [1]  8 25

title: Understanding grid_weights and the L2(mu) norm
text: MetaHunt represents every study-level function values shared grid x_1, ..., x_G. Distances two functions f h computed weighted L^2 sense: vector w = grid_weights exactly vector weights — one non-negative number per grid point. defines measure mu inner product taken respect . Every routine MetaHunt talks “distance functions” “norm function” — basis hunting, constrained projection, conformal pointwise scores — uses grid_weights supply.
code: ||f - h||^2  =  sum_j  w_j * ( f(x_j) - h(x_j) )^2 <f, h>_{L^2(mu)}  =  sum_j  w_j * f(x_j) * h(x_j)

title: Understanding grid_weights and the L2(mu) norm
text: default grid_weights = NULL, inside package becomes uniform vector rep(1 / G, G). Every grid point counts equally. right choice grid representative sample population whose functional care . example, sub-sampled grid held-target-population cohort build_grid(), empirical distribution grid already encodes target measure, uniform grid_weights simply averages empirical distribution. short: grid sampled population P want distances reflect P, leave grid_weights = NULL.

title: Understanding grid_weights and the L2(mu) norm
text: override default grid distribution match population whose distances care . Two common scenarios: grid sampled source population (public reference like NHANES) want distances reflect target population. Pass grid_weights proportional target density evaluated grid point. care sub-region covariate space (e.g. patients age > 65). Set grid_weights[j] 0 outside sub-region target density inside. grid_weights need sum 1 — relative magnitudes matter inside dfspa() project_to_simplex(). default wrapper apply_wrapper() divides sum(grid_weights), weighted means come natural scale regardless normalisation.

what: A worked comparison: uniform vs non-uniform
title: Understanding grid_weights and the L2(mu) norm
text: generate data twice — m = 40 studies, G = 30 grid points — run dfspa() two different grid_weights. first run uniform; second -weights right half grid factor 5. Now two grid_weights choices: single overlay plot two recovered first basis functions makes qualitative effect visible — right-weighted run pays attention right half grid ranking study “extreme” one anchor:  two curves usually agree closely well-sampled regions diverge weights differ . takeaway one choice right wrong — grid_weights quietly determines features function get prioritised.
code: m <- 40 G <- 30 x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = G)  # True bases on the grid. basis <- rbind(sin(pi * x), cos(pi * x), x)  # Mixing weights driven by two latent covariates. W <- data.frame(w1 = rnorm(m), w2 = rnorm(m)) beta <- cbind(c(1, -0.8), c(-0.5, 1.2), c(0, 0)) eta  <- as.matrix(W) %*% beta pi_true <- exp(eta) / rowSums(exp(eta))  F_hat <- pi_true %*% basis + matrix(rnorm(m * G, sd = 0.05), m, G) dim(F_hat) #> [1] 40 30 gw_uniform <- rep(1 / G, G)  # Up-weight x > 0.5 by ~5x. Renormalisation is optional but tidy. gw_right   <- ifelse(x > 0.5, 5, 1) gw_right   <- gw_right / sum(gw_right) * G   # comparable scale, optional  fit_unif  <- dfspa(F_hat, K = 3, grid_weights = gw_uniform, denoise = FALSE) fit_right <- dfspa(F_hat, K = 3, grid_weights = gw_right,   denoise = FALSE)  # Index sets selected can differ. fit_unif$original_indices #> [1] 28 18 14 fit_right$original_indices #> [1] 18 28 14 matplot(   x,   cbind(fit_unif$bases[1, ], fit_right$bases[1, ]),   type = "l", lty = 1, lwd = 2,   col  = c("#0072B2", "#D55E00"),   xlab = "x",   ylab = "first recovered basis",   main = expression(     "Recovered first-vertex basis under uniform vs. target-weighted " ~ L^2 * (mu)   ) ) abline(v = 0.5, lty = 2, col = "grey60") legend("topright",        legend = c("uniform", "up-weight x > 0.5", "x = 0.5 boundary"),        col    = c("#0072B2", "#D55E00", "grey60"),        lty = c(1, 1, 2), lwd = c(2, 2, 1), bty = "n")

title: Scalar summaries with wrapper
text: simulate multi-site clinical trial m = 8 sites. site individual-level data (Y,X,T)(Y, X, T) YY continuous outcome, XX single patient covariate (age), TT binary treatment. site-level CATE function τ()(age)=E[Y(1)−Y(0)∣age,site=]\tau^{()}(\text{age}) = E[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid \text{age}, \text{site} = ] varies across sites way depends site’s metadata. site fits grf::causal_forest individual-level data, shares fitted model — patient data — us. site fits causal_forest. use num.trees = 200 keep vignette fast; practice use default 2000 . stack per-site CATE estimates shared age grid m--G matrix F_hat. pass explicit predict_fn illustrate general pattern; dispatch table inside f_hat_from_models() already knows call causal_forest, users standard grf::causal_forest, default predict_fn sufficient can omit predict_fn argument. now fit metahunt() (F_hat, W) ask predicted ATE hypothetical new site. scalar ate_pred predicted average treatment effect hypothetical new site metadata (year = 2018, pct_treated = 0.45), taking unweighted mean 30-point age grid.
code: m <- 8 n_per_site <- 200 G <- 30  W <- data.frame(   year        = sample(2010:2020, m, replace = TRUE),   pct_treated = round(runif(m, 0.3, 0.6), 2) )  site_data_list <- lapply(seq_len(m), function(i) {   age <- runif(n_per_site, 30, 80)   T   <- rbinom(n_per_site, 1, W$pct_treated[i])   site_eff <- (W$year[i] - 2015) / 5   # site-level shift in CATE   tau_age  <- 0.02 * (age - 50) + site_eff   Y0  <- 0.01 * age + rnorm(n_per_site, sd = 0.5)   Y1  <- Y0 + tau_age   Y   <- ifelse(T == 1, Y1, Y0)   data.frame(Y = Y, age = age, T = T) })  grid <- data.frame(age = seq(30, 80, length.out = G)) cf_models <- lapply(site_data_list, function(d)   grf::causal_forest(X = matrix(d$age, ncol = 1),                      Y = d$Y,                      W = d$T,                      num.trees = 200)) cate_predict <- function(model, grid) {   as.numeric(stats::predict(model, newdata = matrix(grid$age, ncol = 1))$predictions) } F_hat <- f_hat_from_models(cf_models, grid, predict_fn = cate_predict) dim(F_hat) #> [1]  8 30 fit <- metahunt(F_hat, W, K = 3, dfspa_args = list(denoise = FALSE)) W_new <- data.frame(year = 2018, pct_treated = 0.45) ate_pred <- predict(fit, newdata = W_new, wrapper = mean) ate_pred #> [1] 0.9247137

title: MetaHunt — Privacy-Preserving Meta-Analysis via Low-Rank Basis Hunting
text: MetaHunt R package privacy-preserving meta-analysis function-valued quantities (e.g. regression functions, conditional average treatment effect functions) across heterogeneous studies (Shi, Imai, & Zhang 2024, arXiv:2604.23847). Documentation: https://wshi18.github.io/MetaHunt/ package implements methodology Shi, W., Imai, K., Zhang, Y. Privacy-preserving Meta-analysis Low-Rank Basis Hunting. Given aggregate-level estimates function interest several source studies, together study-level covariates, MetaHunt recovers small set latent basis functions span studies low-rank assumption, models mixing weights depend study-level covariates via Dirichlet regression, predicts corresponding function new target population valid conformal prediction intervals. Individual-level data source studies required, makes procedure suitable federated multi-site settings. package also exports minmax_regret(), covariate-free baseline based worst-case-regret aggregator Zhang, Huang, Imai (2024, arXiv:2412.11136). useful number source studies small study-level covariates uninformative.

title: MetaHunt — Privacy-Preserving Meta-Analysis via Low-Rank Basis Hunting
text: See vignette("metahunt-intro") full walkthrough including data preparation fitted model objects, rank selection, conformal intervals (split / cross / pre-fit), minimax-regret baseline.
code: library(MetaHunt) set.seed(1)  # Simulate m studies, each represented by its function on a shared grid. G <- 30; m <- 60 x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = G) basis <- rbind(sin(pi * x), cos(pi * x), x) W <- data.frame(w1 = rnorm(m), w2 = rnorm(m)) eta <- as.matrix(W) %*% cbind(c(1, -0.5), c(-0.4, 1), c(0, 0)) pi_true <- exp(eta) / rowSums(exp(eta)) F_hat <- pi_true %*% basis + matrix(rnorm(m * G, sd = 0.05), m, G)  # Fit and predict for a new study with W_new = (0, 0). fit   <- metahunt(F_hat, W, K = 3, dfspa_args = list(denoise = FALSE)) W_new <- data.frame(w1 = 0, w2 = 0) pred  <- predict(fit, newdata = W_new, wrapper = mean)  # 95% conformal interval for the same target. ci <- split_conformal(F_hat, W, W_new, K = 3,                       wrapper = mean, alpha = 0.05, seed = 1,                       dfspa_args = list(denoise = FALSE)) ci$prediction c(ci$lower, ci$upper)

title: Reduce predicted functions to scalars via a user-supplied wrapper — apply_wrapper
code: F <- matrix(1:12, nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)  # 3 "functions" on a 4-point grid apply_wrapper(F)                            # row means (uniform grid weights) #> [1]  2.5  6.5 10.5 apply_wrapper(F, wrapper = max)             # row maxes #> [1]  4  8 12 apply_wrapper(F, wrapper = function(f) f[2]) # point evaluation at grid idx 2 #> [1]  2  6 10

title: Extract coefficients from a MetaHunt weight model — coef.metahunt_weight_model
code: # \donttest{ set.seed(1) m <- 60; K <- 3 W <- data.frame(w1 = rnorm(m), w2 = rnorm(m)) eta <- cbind(0.5 * W$w1, -0.3 * W$w2, rep(0, m)) pi_true <- exp(eta) / rowSums(exp(eta)) pi_hat <- pi_true + matrix(rnorm(m * K, sd = 0.01), m, K) pi_hat <- pmax(pi_hat, 0); pi_hat <- pi_hat / rowSums(pi_hat) model <- fit_weight_model(pi_hat, W) coef(model) #> $v1 #> (Intercept)          w1          w2  #>   6.7709026   0.7037691   0.1620994  #>  #> $v2 #> (Intercept)          w1          w2  #>   6.7695064   0.1975436  -0.1387985  #>  #> $v3 #> (Intercept)          w1          w2  #>   6.7736531   0.2093901   0.1684880  #>  # }

title: Split conformal intervals from a pre-fit MetaHunt pipeline — conformal_from_fit
code: set.seed(1) G <- 30; m <- 80 x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = G) basis <- rbind(sin(pi * x), cos(pi * x), x) W <- data.frame(w1 = rnorm(m)) eta <- cbind(0.8 * W$w1, -0.4 * W$w1, rep(0, m)) pi_true <- exp(eta) / rowSums(exp(eta)) F_hat <- pi_true %*% basis + matrix(rnorm(m * G, sd = 0.05), m, G)  # user-controlled split and fit tr  <- 1:50; cal <- 51:70; new <- 71:80 fit <- dfspa(F_hat[tr, ], K = 3) pih <- project_to_simplex(F_hat[tr, ], fit$bases) wm  <- fit_weight_model(pih, W[tr, , drop = FALSE])  res <- conformal_from_fit(   fit, wm,   F_cal = F_hat[cal, ], W_cal = W[cal, , drop = FALSE],   W_new = W[new, , drop = FALSE],   wrapper = mean ) res #> MetaHunt conformal prediction #>   method:        split  #>   alpha:         0.05  #>   n calibration: 20  #>   mode:          scalar (via wrapper) #>   n targets:     10  #>   quantile:      0.1492

title: Empirical coverage of a conformal prediction-interval object — coverage
code: set.seed(1) G <- 25; m <- 80 x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = G) basis <- rbind(sin(pi * x), cos(pi * x), x) W <- data.frame(w1 = rnorm(m)) eta <- cbind(0.6 * W$w1, -0.3 * W$w1, rep(0, m)) pi_true <- exp(eta) / rowSums(exp(eta)) F_hat <- pi_true %*% basis + matrix(rnorm(m * G, sd = 0.05), m, G)  # held-out test set: same data-generating process, same W test_idx <- 1:10 train_idx <- setdiff(seq_len(m), test_idx) res <- split_conformal(F_hat[train_idx, ], W[train_idx, , drop = FALSE],                        W[test_idx, , drop = FALSE], K = 3,                        dfspa_args = list(denoise = FALSE), seed = 1) cov <- coverage(res, F_obs = F_hat[test_idx, , drop = FALSE]) cov$overall #> [1] 0.964

Default 5. wrapper Optional reduction function (see apply_wrapper()). NULL, intervals constructed pointwise every grid point. grid_weights Optional length-G_grid non-negative numeric vector used \(L^2(\mu)\) norm default wrapper. dfspa_args, weight_model_args Named lists passed dfspa() fit_weight_model() respectively. seed Optional integer seed reproducible train/calibration splits.

title: Cross-conformal prediction intervals (pooled K-fold scores) — cross_conformal
text: fold, MetaHunt pipeline fit --fold studies, conformity scores computed -fold studies. folds complete, pooled scores yield single \((1-\alpha)\)-quantile (one per grid point). Point predictions W_new use pipeline refit full dataset. interval grid point g target j \([\tilde f^{(j)}(x_g) - q_g, \tilde f^{(j)}(x_g) + q_g]\) (scalar analogue wrapper supplied). differs Vovk's original cross-conformal predictor classification. regression, pooling scores across folds common practical extension split conformal reduces variance due single calibration split. title: Cross-conformal prediction intervals (pooled K-fold scores) — cross_conformal
code: # \donttest{ set.seed(1) G <- 30; m <- 60; K_true <- 3 x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = G) basis <- rbind(sin(pi * x), cos(pi * x), x) W <- data.frame(w1 = rnorm(m)) eta <- cbind(0.8 * W$w1, -0.3 * W$w1, rep(0, m)) pi_true <- exp(eta) / rowSums(exp(eta)) F_hat <- pi_true %*% basis + matrix(rnorm(m * G, sd = 0.05), m, G) W_new <- data.frame(w1 = c(0, 1))  res <- cross_conformal(F_hat, W, W_new, K = 3, n_folds = 4,                        dfspa_args = list(denoise = FALSE), seed = 1) res #> MetaHunt conformal prediction #>   method:        cross  #>   alpha:         0.05  #>   n calibration: 60  #>   mode:          pointwise (per grid point) #>   n targets:     2  #>   grid size:     30  #>   quantile range: 0.1011 to 0.2328  # }

title: Cross-validated prediction-error curve for basis-rank selection — cv_error_curve
code: # \donttest{ set.seed(1) G <- 40; m <- 80; K_true <- 3 x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = G) basis <- rbind(sin(pi * x), cos(pi * x), x) W <- data.frame(w1 = rnorm(m), w2 = rnorm(m)) eta <- as.matrix(W) %*% cbind(c(1, -0.8), c(-0.5, 1.2), c(0, 0)) pi_true <- exp(eta) / rowSums(exp(eta)) F_hat <- pi_true %*% basis + matrix(rnorm(m * G, sd = 0.02), m, G) cv <- cv_error_curve(F_hat, W, K_range = 2:5, n_folds = 4, seed = 1) cv #>   K   cv_error       cv_se #> 1 2 0.08336472 0.003590282 #> 2 3 0.06120156 0.007567407 #> 3 4 0.10205196 0.012299971 #> 4 5 0.11146587 0.012849123 # }

what: Denoised functional Successive Projection Algorithm (d-fSPA) — dfspa
title: Denoised functional Successive Projection Algorithm (d-fSPA) — dfspa
text: Recovers set K latent basis functions collection study-level function estimates low-rank cross-study heterogeneity assumption Shi, Imai, Zhang. Implements Algorithm 1 paper ("d-fSPA Algorithm basis hunting").

title: Denoised functional Successive Projection Algorithm (d-fSPA) — dfspa
code: dfspa(F_hat, K, grid_weights = NULL, N = NULL, Delta = NULL, denoise = TRUE)

title: Denoised functional Successive Projection Algorithm (d-fSPA) — dfspa
text: F_hat m--G numeric matrix row evaluation estimated function \(\hat f^{()}\) G grid points. K Integer number basis functions recover. Must satisfy 1 <= K <= m denoising. grid_weights Optional length-G non-negative numeric vector grid weights defining \(L^2(\mu)\) inner product. Defaults uniform weights 1 / G. N, Delta Optional numeric tuning parameters controlling denoising. See Details. denoise Logical; FALSE, denoising step skipped plain fSPA run. Defaults TRUE.

title: Denoised functional Successive Projection Algorithm (d-fSPA) — dfspa
text: object class "dfspa": list containing bases K--G matrix whose rows recovered basis functions evaluated grid (denoised, applicable). selected Length-K integer vector selected row indices post-denoising function matrix. original_indices Length-K integer vector selected study indices original input F_hat (rows dropped denoising). kept Integer vector row indices F_hat survived denoising. F_denoised post-denoising function matrix (length(kept)--G). grid_weights Grid weights used. N, Delta Tuning parameters actually used (NA denoise = FALSE). K Number bases requested. call matched call.

title: Denoised functional Successive Projection Algorithm (d-fSPA) — dfspa
text: study-level function represented evaluations shared grid G points. (weighted) \(L^2(\mu)\) inner product \(\langle f,g\rangle = \sum_{j=1}^G w_j f(x_j) g(x_j)\), grid_weights w_j proportional measure \(\mu\). supplied, uniform weights 1 / G used. Denoising follows Jin (2024): study , let \(B_\Delta(\hat f^{()}) = \{j : \|\hat f^{(j)} - \hat f^{()}\| \le \Delta\}\). \(|B_\Delta(\hat f^{()})| < N\), study discarded; otherwise \(\hat f^{()}\) replaced average functions \(B_\Delta\). denoising, functional SPA step iteratively selects, K iterations, remaining function largest norm projecting span previously selected bases. Default tuning parameters follow heuristics paper: N = 0.5 * log(m) \(\Delta = \max_{ij} \|\hat f^{()} - \hat f^{(j)}\| / 10\).

title: Denoised functional Successive Projection Algorithm (d-fSPA) — dfspa
code: set.seed(1) G <- 50 x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = G) basis <- rbind(sin(pi * x), cos(pi * x), x)          # 3 true bases pi_mat <- rbind(diag(3),                              # 3 pure studies                 c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2),                 c(0.2, 0.5, 0.3),                 c(0.3, 0.3, 0.4)) F_hat <- pi_mat %*% basis                             # m = 6, G = 50 fit <- dfspa(F_hat, K = 3, denoise = FALSE) fit$original_indices    # should be a permutation of 1, 2, 3 #> [1] 2 1 3

code: # Toy example: each "centre" fits a polynomial regression set.seed(1) make_centre_data <- function(slope) {   x <- runif(60)   data.frame(x = x, y = slope * x + rnorm(60, sd = 0.1)) } models <- lapply(c(-1, 0, 1, 0.5, -0.5), function(s)   stats::lm(y ~ poly(x, 2), data = make_centre_data(s)))  grid  <- data.frame(x = seq(0, 1, length.out = 30)) F_hat <- f_hat_from_models(models, grid) dim(F_hat)   # 5 x 30 #> [1]  5 30

title: Fit a weight model mapping study-level covariates to simplex weights — fit_weight_model
code: # \donttest{ set.seed(1) m <- 80; K <- 3; p <- 2 W <- matrix(rnorm(m * p), m, p); colnames(W) <- c("w1", "w2") # generate simplex weights driven by W eta <- cbind(0.5 * W[, 1], -0.3 * W[, 2], rep(0, m)) pi_true <- exp(eta) / rowSums(exp(eta)) pi_hat <- pi_true + matrix(rnorm(m * K, sd = 0.01), m, K) pi_hat <- pmax(pi_hat, 0); pi_hat <- pi_hat / rowSums(pi_hat) model <- fit_weight_model(pi_hat, W) predict(model, newdata = matrix(c(0, 0), 1, 2, dimnames = list(NULL, c("w1","w2")))) #>           [,1]      [,2]      [,3] #> [1,] 0.3328722 0.3340615 0.3330662 # }

code: set.seed(1) G <- 40; m <- 80 x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = G) basis <- rbind(sin(pi * x), cos(pi * x), x) W <- data.frame(w1 = rnorm(m), w2 = rnorm(m)) eta <- as.matrix(W) %*% cbind(c(1, -0.8), c(-0.5, 1.2), c(0, 0)) pi_true <- exp(eta) / rowSums(exp(eta)) F_hat <- pi_true %*% basis + matrix(rnorm(m * G, sd = 0.05), m, G)  fit <- metahunt(F_hat, W, K = 3) fit #> MetaHunt fit #>   m (studies):    80  #>   G (grid size):  40  #>   K (bases):      3  #>   weight method:  dirichlet  #>   predictors:     w1, w2  f_pred <- predict(fit, newdata = W[1:3, ]) dim(f_pred)                                  # 3 x G: predicted functions #> [1]  3 40 predict(fit, newdata = W[1:3, ], wrapper = mean)  # scalar summaries #> [1] 0.39676714 0.38963182 0.08180082

what: Minimax-regret aggregator for multisite function-valued estimands — minmax_regret
title: Minimax-regret aggregator for multisite function-valued estimands — minmax_regret
text: Implements minimax-regret estimator Zhang, Huang, Imai (arXiv:2412.11136) aggregating site-level function estimates. Given study-level functions \(\hat f^{()}\), estimator $$\hat q = \arg\min_{q \\Delta_{m-1}}\   q^\top \hat\Gamma\, q - \hat d^\top q,   \qquad   \hat\Gamma_{ij} = \sum_g w_g\, \hat f^{()}(x_g)\hat f^{(j)}(x_g),\ \   \hat d_i = \sum_g w_g\, (\hat f^{()}(x_g))^2,$$ yielding predicted target function \(\tilde f(x) = \sum_{=1}^m \hat q_i\, \hat f^{()}(x)\). Unlike metahunt(), method use study-level covariates; target worst-case-regret aggregator convex hull source functions.

title: Project study-level functions onto the simplex spanned by basis functions — project_to_simplex
text: study , solves constrained projection $$\hat\pi_i = \arg\min_{\pi \\Delta_{K-1}}   \left\| \hat f^{()} - \sum_{k=1}^K \pi_k \hat g_k \right\|_{L^2(\mu)}$$ norm weighted \(L^2\) norm defined grid_weights. Equation (3) paper yields study-specific weights hat pi_i used downstream weight-model fitting prediction.

title: Project study-level functions onto the simplex spanned by basis functions — project_to_simplex
text: F_hat m--G_grid numeric matrix; row study function \(\hat f^{()}\) evaluated shared grid. bases K--G_grid numeric matrix basis functions grid, typically bases slot dfspa() result. grid_weights Optional length-G_grid non-negative numeric vector grid weights defining \(L^2(\mu)\) inner product. Defaults uniform weights 1 / G_grid. ridge Small non-negative scalar added diagonal QP Hessian numerical stability. Defaults 1e-10.

title: Project study-level functions onto the simplex spanned by basis functions — project_to_simplex
text: m--K numeric matrix simplex weights; rows sum 1 entries non-negative.

title: Project study-level functions onto the simplex spanned by basis functions — project_to_simplex
text: projection reduces quadratic program $$\min_{\pi \\mathbb R^K}\ \pi^\top D\,\pi - 2\,d^\top \pi   \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \mathbf 1^\top \pi = 1,\ \pi \ge 0$$ \(D = G W G^\top\) \(d = G W f^{()}\), \(G\) K--G_grid basis matrix, \(W = \mathrm{diag}(\)grid_weights\()\), \(f^{()}\) -th row F_hat. Solved via quadprog::solve.QP(). tiny ridge added D numerical stability.

title: Project study-level functions onto the simplex spanned by basis functions — project_to_simplex
code: set.seed(1) G <- 40 x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = G) basis <- rbind(sin(pi * x), cos(pi * x), x) true_pi <- rbind(diag(3), c(0.4, 0.3, 0.3), c(0.1, 0.7, 0.2)) F_hat <- true_pi %*% basis fit <- dfspa(F_hat, K = 3, denoise = FALSE) pi_hat <- project_to_simplex(F_hat, fit$bases) round(pi_hat, 3) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]  0.0  1.0  0.0 #> [2,]  1.0  0.0  0.0 #> [3,]  0.0  0.0  1.0 #> [4,]  0.3  0.4  0.3 #> [5,]  0.7  0.1  0.2

title: Split conformal prediction intervals for target-function predictions — split_conformal
text: Given target function, one can either construct intervals pointwise every grid point (wrapper = NULL) scalar summary target function (wrapper function). Pointwise (wrapper = NULL): grid point g conformity score \(R_{,g} = |\hat f^{()}(x_g) - \tilde   f^{()}(x_g)|\) across calibration studies . separate \((1-\alpha)\)-quantile \(q_g\) computed per grid point, interval grid point g target j \([\tilde f^{(j)}(x_g)-q_g,\ \tilde f^{(j)}(x_g)+q_g]\). Scalar (wrapper supplied): conformity scores \(R_i = |s(\hat f^{()}) - s(\tilde f^{()})|\) s = wrapper, interval target \([s(\tilde f^{(j)})-q,\ s(\tilde f^{(j)})+q]\) single shared quantile \(q\). finite-sample quantile \(q = R_{(k)}\) \(k = \lceil(1-\alpha)(n_\mathrm{cal}+1)\rceil\); \(k > n_\mathrm{cal}\), q = Inf intervals \((-\infty, \infty)\).

title: Split conformal prediction intervals for target-function predictions — split_conformal
code: set.seed(1) G <- 40; m <- 80; K_true <- 3 x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = G) basis <- rbind(sin(pi * x), cos(pi * x), x) W <- data.frame(w1 = rnorm(m), w2 = rnorm(m)) eta <- as.matrix(W) %*% cbind(c(1, -0.8), c(-0.5, 1.2), c(0, 0)) pi_true <- exp(eta) / rowSums(exp(eta)) F_hat <- pi_true %*% basis + matrix(rnorm(m * G, sd = 0.05), m, G)  W_new <- data.frame(w1 = c(0, 1), w2 = c(0, -1)) # pointwise intervals at every grid point pi_grid <- split_conformal(F_hat, W, W_new, K = 3, seed = 1) dim(pi_grid$lower)  # 2 x 40 #> [1]  2 40 # scalar intervals for the grid-weighted mean (ATE-style) pi_ate  <- split_conformal(F_hat, W, W_new, K = 3, wrapper = mean, seed = 1) pi_ate$prediction #> [1] 0.3668234 0.5851352

title: Summarise a MetaHunt fit — summary.metahunt
code: set.seed(1) G <- 25; m <- 40 x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = G) basis <- rbind(sin(pi * x), cos(pi * x), x) W <- data.frame(w1 = rnorm(m), w2 = rnorm(m)) eta <- as.matrix(W) %*% cbind(c(1, -0.8), c(-0.5, 1.2), c(0, 0)) pi_true <- exp(eta) / rowSums(exp(eta)) F_hat <- pi_true %*% basis + matrix(rnorm(m * G, sd = 0.05), m, G)  fit <- metahunt(F_hat, W, K = 3, dfspa_args = list(denoise = FALSE)) summary(fit) #> MetaHunt fit summary #>   m (studies):    40  #>   G (grid size):  25  #>   K (bases):      3  #>   weight method:  dirichlet  #>   predictors:     w1, w2  #>   studies kept:   40  #>   studies dropped: 0  #>   denoising N:    NA  #>   denoising Delta: NA  #>  #> Per-basis pi_hat summary: #>         min   mean median max     sd #> basis_1   0 0.3099 0.2376   1 0.2845 #> basis_2   0 0.3117 0.1956   1 0.3204 #> basis_3   0 0.3784 0.3983   1 0.2214

title: Summarise a conformal prediction-interval object — summary.metahunt_conformal
code: set.seed(1) G <- 25; m <- 60 x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = G) basis <- rbind(sin(pi * x), cos(pi * x), x) W <- data.frame(w1 = rnorm(m)) eta <- cbind(0.6 * W$w1, -0.3 * W$w1, rep(0, m)) pi_true <- exp(eta) / rowSums(exp(eta)) F_hat <- pi_true %*% basis + matrix(rnorm(m * G, sd = 0.05), m, G) W_new <- data.frame(w1 = c(0, 1)) res <- split_conformal(F_hat, W, W_new, K = 3,                        dfspa_args = list(denoise = FALSE), seed = 1) #> Warning: With n_cal = 18 and alpha = 0.05, the conformal quantile is infinite at 25 of 25 grid points; intervals are unbounded there. Increase calibration size (raise `cal_frac` or supply more studies) or use a larger `alpha`. summary(res) #> Summary of MetaHunt conformal prediction #>   method:         split  #>   alpha:          0.05  #>   n calibration:  18  #>   mode:           pointwise (per grid point) #>   n targets:      2  #>   grid size:      25  #>   mean interval width: NA  #>   fraction finite quantiles: 0  #>   quantile:       all infinite (insufficient calibration)

text: split_conformal() cross_conformal() return distribution-free prediction intervals around target function (pointwise grid , wrapper, around scalar summary). conformal_from_fit() adds intervals already-fit pipeline using held-calibration set. coverage(), summary(), plot() methods "metahunt_conformal" class.

title: MetaHunt 0.1.0
text: dfspa() denoised functional Successive Projection Algorithm (Algorithm 1 paper). project_to_simplex() constrained simplex projection study’s function onto recovered bases (quadratic program via quadprog). fit_weight_model() predict.metahunt_weight_model() Dirichlet regression simplex weights study-level covariates, coef.metahunt_weight_model() inspecting coefficients. predict_target() apply_wrapper() composing predictions scalar summaries hand.

Custom S4 classes can supply predict_fn.
